title: Jobs Now Act of 2025
plain_summary: The Jobs Now Act of 2025 creates a two-year test program run by the Department of Labor to help save and create public service jobs. The program gives money to local governments and community groups, with at least half of the funding used to keep workers who might lose their jobs due to budget cuts. The program especially encourages hiring veterans, people with disabilities, those getting unemployment benefits, and workers who lost their previous jobs. This pilot program aims to help areas with high unemployment and poverty by supporting important public service positions.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.
subjects: Government employee pay, benefits, personnel management

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Jobs Now Act of two thousand twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2  Grants to Units of General Local Government
sections.text:
This section changes an existing job training law to create a new two year test program. The program will give money to local governments and community groups to help create and keep jobs that serve the public.

A unit of general local government means any city, county, or other local government area that can collect taxes and spend money for public services. This includes places like cities and counties in states, as well as territories like the Virgin Islands, Guam, American Samoa, and the Northern Mariana Islands.

Local governments and community groups must use at least half of their grant money to keep employees who would otherwise lose their jobs because of budget cuts. These must be employees who provide public services. If half the grant money is more than needed to save these jobs, they only need to use what is actually needed.

After saving existing jobs, local governments and community groups can use leftover money to hire new people for public service jobs or train people for these positions. Community groups can also use money to hire or train people for private sector jobs.

The Secretary (the government official in charge of this program) should encourage grant recipients to focus on helping veterans, people with disabilities, people getting unemployment benefits, and workers who lost their jobs when companies closed or moved.

When choosing who gets grants, the Secretary must give priority to local governments and community groups in areas with high unemployment, high home foreclosure rates, and high poverty rates compared to other areas applying for grants.

Local governments and community groups that want grants must submit applications with information required by the Secretary.

Within two years after money is first set aside for this program, the Secretary must report to Congress about how many people were hired or trained and how many employees kept their jobs because of these grants. The report must also include the best ways to run programs that hire, train, or keep local government employees.

Congress authorizes spending up to one billion dollars for this program for fiscal years two thousand twenty six and two thousand twenty seven.

The section also makes a small change to update the table of contents in the original law to include this new pilot program.
